senses.definition: Additional buildings or rooms that are joined to or near a main building.
examples: The school built new annexes to provide more classrooms.
Guests are staying in the hotel annexes behind the main building.

senses.definition: Extra sections added at the end of formal documents such as reports or contracts.
examples: Full technical data can be found in annexes A and B of the report.
The agreement includes several annexes listing detailed conditions.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of annex: to take control of a country or region and make it part of your own country.
examples: The powerful state often annexes nearby territories after a conflict.
A larger country sometimes annexes land from its weaker neighbors.

senses.definition: The act of taking control of a country or region and making it part of your own country.
examples: The kingdom was annexing neighboring lands to expand its power.
Critics argued that annexing the area violated international law.

senses.definition: The act of adding something, such as a room or document part, to something larger.
examples: They are annexing two new wings to the hospital building.
The committee is annexing a summary to the main report.

senses.definition: An older spelling of annexation, meaning the act of taking a region and making it part of a state.
examples: The annexion of the territory was widely condemned in Europe.
Historians still debate the causes of the annexion of the island.

senses.definition: Someone who supports the policy of taking new territories into a state.
examples: The annexionist argued that expansion was necessary for national security.
Annexionist politicians pushed for control of the neighboring province.

senses.definition: Supporting or relating to the policy of adding new territory to a state.
examples: Annexionist ideas were popular in some circles at the time.
The party adopted an openly annexionist program.

senses.definition: Plural of annexion, an older spelling of annexations, meaning acts of taking territories into a state.
examples: Nineteenth-century Europe saw several annexions of small states.
The report lists past annexions that still affect current borders.

senses.definition: Inflammation of the female reproductive organs next to the uterus, such as the fallopian tubes and ovaries.
examples: Chronic annexitis can cause long-term pelvic pain.
The patient was diagnosed with annexitis and started antibiotic treatment.

senses.definition: Relating to annexing or serving as an addition to something else.
examples: The annexive clauses were added at the end of the contract.
An annexive element can change the meaning of the basic phrase.

senses.definition: The act or process of annexing something, especially land or territory.
examples: The annexment of the region led to years of conflict.
Citizens protested the annexment of their town into the city.

senses.definition: Plural of annexment, meaning acts of annexing land or territory.
examples: The empire's history is full of annexments and treaties.
Several annexments changed the map of the region.

senses.definition: An official document or section added to the end of a main document.
examples: Please see annexure 1 for the full financial details.
Supporting documents are listed in annexure B of the contract.

senses.definition: Plural of annexure, referring to official documents or sections added to a main document.
examples: All annexures must be initialed by both parties.
The policy includes several annexures explaining the procedures.

senses.definition: A familiar short form of the female given name Ann or Annie.
examples: Anni invited us all to her birthday party.
I met Anni when we were studying abroad.

senses.definition: A low dam built across a river, especially in South Asia, to raise the water level and direct water to irrigation channels.
examples: The farmers depend on the old annicut for irrigation water.
A new annicut was constructed to control the flow of the river.

senses.definition: Plural of annicut, meaning low dams across rivers used mainly for irrigation.
examples: Several annicuts were built along the river to support farming.
The flood damaged two of the old annicuts.

senses.definition: A chemical compound, historically mentioned in chemistry, whose specific common use is rare today.
examples: The researcher briefly referred to annidalin in his review of old studies.
Annidalin appears only in a few specialized chemistry texts.

senses.definition: A common female given name.
examples: Annie plays the guitar in a local band.
I met Annie at the conference last year.

senses.definition: A family of legless amphibians, known as worm lizards, mentioned in scientific classification.
examples: The genus Anniella belongs to the family Anniellidae.
Anniellidae are studied mainly by specialists in herpetology.

senses.definition: An obsolete or shortened form of annihilate, meaning to destroy completely.
examples: The old text uses the verb "annihil" to describe total ruin.
In that dialect, they would say the army would annihil its foes.

senses.definition: The quality of being able to be completely destroyed or removed.
examples: The concept of annihilability raises philosophical questions about existence.
Physicists discussed the annihilability of certain particles.

senses.definition: Able to be completely destroyed or removed.
examples: According to this theory, the soul is annihilable and not naturally immortal.
The model treats certain particles as annihilable under specific conditions.

senses.definition: To destroy something completely so that nothing is left.
examples: The bomb could annihilate the entire city.
The new team completely annihilated their opponents in the final.

senses.definition: In physics, for a particle and its antiparticle to disappear and be changed into energy.
examples: When matter and antimatter meet, they annihilate each other.
The particles annihilate to produce gamma rays.

senses.definition: Completely destroyed, with almost nothing remaining.
examples: The town lay annihilated after the earthquake.
His confidence was annihilated by the harsh criticism.

senses.definition: Past tense of annihilate: destroyed something completely.
examples: The explosion annihilated several buildings.
Our team annihilated the opposition in the first half.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of annihilate: destroys something completely.
examples: The virus quickly annihilates healthy cells.
A single mistake sometimes annihilates years of hard work.

senses.definition: Destroying something completely so that nothing is left.
examples: The army was annihilating every sign of resistance.
The fire spread rapidly, annihilating homes in minutes.

senses.definition: Extremely strong and damaging, especially describing criticism or defeat.
examples: She faced annihilating criticism after the failed project.
The team suffered an annihilating defeat in the final.

senses.definition: The act of destroying something completely so that nothing is left.
examples: They feared the total annihilation of their culture.
Nuclear war could lead to the annihilation of humanity.

senses.definition: In physics, the process in which a particle and its antiparticle disappear and are changed into energy.
examples: Electron–positron annihilation produces gamma rays.
The experiment measured the products of particle annihilation.

senses.definition: The Christian belief that the wicked will finally be destroyed and will not suffer forever in hell.
examples: Annihilationism teaches that the lost will eventually cease to exist rather than suffer eternal torment.
Some modern theologians defend annihilationism as more consistent with God's love.

senses.definition: A person who believes or teaches annihilationism in Christian theology.
examples: The annihilationist argued that eternal punishment is inconsistent with divine justice.
Some early Adventist writers were strong annihilationists.

senses.definition: Relating to or supporting the belief that the wicked will finally be destroyed instead of suffering forever.
examples: He presented an annihilationist reading of the New Testament texts.
Their church has moved toward an annihilationist position on hell.

senses.definition: Having the character of annihilationism or strongly supporting the idea that the wicked will be destroyed rather than suffer forever.
examples: The author took a clearly annihilationistic approach to the doctrine of hell.
Critics attacked his annihilationistic reinterpretation of traditional beliefs.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to or supports annihilationism.
examples: He argued annihilationistical ly in favor of a final end to the wicked.
The doctrine was almost annihilationistical in its conclusions.

senses.definition: Plural of annihilation, referring to acts of complete destruction.
examples: Wars and genocides are tragic annihilations of human life.
Future conflicts could lead to mutual annihilations.

senses.definition: Causing or able to cause complete destruction.
examples: They feared the annihilative power of modern weapons.
His criticism had an almost annihilative effect on her confidence.

senses.definition: A person or thing that destroys something completely.
examples: The new weapon was described as a city annihilator in the press.
In the story, time acts as the great annihilator of all things.

senses.definition: In mathematics, an element or set that sends every element of a structure to zero under a given operation.
examples: In this ring, zero itself is the simplest annihilator.
We can describe the annihilator of this subspace in dual space.

senses.definition: Plural of annihilator: people or things that destroy completely.
examples: They were seen as the annihilators of traditional culture.
Modern weapons can be true annihilators of cities.

senses.definition: Causing or relating to complete destruction.
examples: The treaty reduced the threat of annihilatory war.
He spoke of the annihilatory tendencies of modern technology.

senses.definition: A rare term that appears in older texts, probably referring to a follower of someone named Ann or Annie.
examples: The text mentioned a small group of Annists in the region.
Each Annist supported the ideas of their leader.

senses.definition: A dark mica mineral containing iron and potassium, found in some rocks.
examples: The rock sample contained annite and several other minerals.
Annite can be identified by its color and crystal structure.

senses.definition: Written short form of anniversary.
examples: They celebrated their 10th wedding anniv last week.
Please write the event date next to the anniv entry.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to or happens on an anniversary.
examples: The event was celebrated anniversalily each year.
The custom is observed anniversalily in the village.

senses.definition: Plural of anniversary, days each year when you remember or celebrate a special event that happened on that date.
examples: They always go out for dinner on their wedding anniversaries.
The city holds parades on the anniversaries of important battles.

senses.definition: In a way that happens each year on the date of a past event.
examples: The ceremony is held anniversarily to remember the victims.
The award is given anniversarily to outstanding students.

senses.definition: The quality of relating to or being like an anniversary.
examples: The anniversariness of the date made the speech more emotional.
She reflected on the anniversariness of the event in her article.

senses.definition: The date each year when you remember or celebrate something special that happened in the past.
examples: They went on a trip for their wedding anniversary.
The company is celebrating its 50th anniversary this year.

senses.definition: An old form of the word anniversary, meaning a yearly return of a special date.
examples: In the old poem, the child's birth anniverse is described with joy.
Writers of that period sometimes used "anniverse" instead of "anniversary".

senses.definition: In the year (used in Latin phrases giving a date).
examples: The document is dated anno 1845.
Anno Domini is often shortened to AD in English dates.

senses.definition: Marked with a date or year, especially using the Latin word anno.
examples: The annodated manuscript shows when it was copied.
Each stone was annodated with the year of construction.

senses.definition: To name or mention by name, especially in an official way.
examples: The statute annominates several earlier acts.
He annominated all parties involved in the agreement.

senses.definition: The act of naming or mentioning something, sometimes with a play on similar-sounding words.
examples: The poet's use of annomination creates a clever sound pattern.
Annunciation and denunciation form an interesting annomination in the text.

senses.definition: Any tree or plant of the tropical genus Annona, which includes sugar apples and custard apples.
examples: The garden contains an annona that produces sweet fruit.
Annona species are grown in many warm countries.

senses.definition: A family of tropical trees and shrubs, including the genus Annona and many fruit-bearing species.
examples: Custard apples belong to the family Annonaceae.
Annonaceae are mainly found in tropical regions.

senses.definition: Belonging to or related to the plant family Annonaceae.
examples: The region is rich in annonaceous plants.
Annonaceous fruits are popular in local markets.
